coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic properties of nanostructured g-C3N4 hollow microspheres decorated with OH-CQDs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Xin
Chen, Jianru
Jiang, Ruobing
Chen, Haiyang
Zhang, Jielin
Ren, Jinlong
Yu, Xihao
Liu, Lina
Feng, Chenchen
Li, Cuixia
Yu, Fucheng
Hu, Kechao
Hou, Xiaogang
coredata.dc:description: Hydroxylated carbon quantum dots (OH-CQDs) modified g-C3N4 hollow microspheres were prepared using a supramolecular precursor self-assembly method. The incorporation of OH-CQDs into the precursor resulted in the formation of a hollow flower-like microsphere structure composed of g-C3N4 nanoflakes in the final samples, which exhibited a morphology that was roughly inherited from that of the supramolecular precursor. The incorporation of an optimal quantity of OH-CQDs did not result in a deterioration of the g-C3N4 product morphology; rather, it led to an enhancement in its microsphere structure. The modified nanostructure of g-C3N4 resulted in a significant increase in reaction sites and promoted the adsorption of organic dye molecules on the surface of the g-C3N4 photocatalyst during degradation reactions. Furthermore, despite the reduction in carrier concentration of g-C3N4 resulting from the decoration with OH-CQDs, the impedance was significantly diminished and the photocurrent density was also markedly improved. It can thus be concluded that the decoration of OH-CQDs has significantly enhanced the mobility of g-C3N4, thereby contributing to the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4 photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4 nanoflakes
Hollow microspheres
OH-CQDs
Composite photocatalyst
Carrier mobility

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of the samples. (a) CN, (b-e) CCN-0, CCN-0.5, CCN-1 and CCN-1.5, and (f, g) High resolution images of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples.
BET measurements of the CCN-x series samples. (a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, and (b) Pore size distribution.
XRD patterns of the CCN-x series samples.

EDXS measurements. (a, b) Local scanning areas of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples, (c-e) Distribution of C, N and O elements in the CCN-0 sample, and (f-h) Distribution of C, N and O elements in the CCN-0.5 sample.
TEM images of the samples. (a) Pure OH-CQDs, (b) CCN-0.5, and (c, d) CCN-0 and CCN-0.5.
XPS measurements of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples. (a) Survey plots, and elemental binding states of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s.

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra of the CCN-x series samples, (b) Optical band gaps of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples, (c, d) Flat band potentials of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples, respectively, and (e)Band structures of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples.
Characterization of the photoelectric properties of the CCN-x series samples. (a) PL measurements, (b) EIS spectra, and (c) Photocurrent response.

Photocatalytic property characterizations of the CCN-x series samples. (a) Photocatalytic performance comparison of the samples, (b) Time-dependent concentration of RhB solution under the photocatalysis of the CCN-0.5 sample, (c) Plots of rate constants for the RhB solution under the photocatalysis of the samples, (d) Contribution of the produced active groups in the degradation of RhB solution under the photocatalysis of the CCN-0.5 sample, (e) Cyclic photocatalytic performance stability of the CCN-0.5 sample, and (f) XRD spectrum comparison of the CCN-0.5 sample before and after cyclic photocatalytic process.

The respond intensity of the active groups in EPR measurements of the CCN-0.5 sample. (a) h+ active groups, (b) e− active groups, (c)
OH radicals, and (d)
O
2
-
radicals.
Schematic diagram of photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the CCN-0.5 sample.
Specific surface area and average pore size of the CCN-x series samples.
FWHM values of the CCN-x series samples.
Concentrations of C, N, and O elements, along with the C/N atomic ratio in the CCN-x series samples.
Relative amounts of C–C/C=C and N-C=N groups, along with their ratios, based on the C 1s binding states observed in the CCN-x series samples.
Relative amounts of N–(C)3 and C–N=C groups, along with their ratios, based on the N 1s binding states observed in the CCN-x series samples.
Reaction rate constant of RhB solution photocatalyzed by the prepared samples.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic properties of nanostructured g-C3N4 hollow microspheres decorated with OH-CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050 Gansu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, Gansu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Nonferrous Metals, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050 Gansu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Processing and Recycling of Nonferrous Metals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lanzhou Lanshi Testing Technology Co., Ltd, Lanzhou 730030 Gansu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lanzhou Lanshi Zhongke Nanotechnology Co., Ltd, Lanzhou 730030 Gansu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lanzhou Lanshi Zhongke Nanotechnology Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lanzhou Lanshi Zhongke Nanotechnology Co., Ltd, Lanzhou 730030, Gansu, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydroxylated carbon quantum dots (OH-CQDs) modified g-C3N4 hollow microspheres were prepared using a supramolecular precursor self-assembly method. The incorporation of OH-CQDs into the precursor resulted in the formation of a hollow flower-like microsphere structure composed of g-C3N4 nanoflakes in the final samples, which exhibited a morphology that was roughly inherited from that of the supramolecular precursor. The incorporation of an optimal quantity of OH-CQDs did not result in a deterioration of the g-C3N4 product morphology; rather, it led to an enhancement in its microsphere structure. The modified nanostructure of g-C3N4 resulted in a significant increase in reaction sites and promoted the adsorption of organic dye molecules on the surface of the g-C3N4 photocatalyst during degradation reactions. Furthermore, despite the reduction in carrier concentration of g-C3N4 resulting from the decoration with OH-CQDs, the impedance was significantly diminished and the photocurrent density was also markedly improved. It can thus be concluded that the decoration of OH-CQDs has significantly enhanced the mobility of g-C3N4, thereby contributing to the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4 photocatalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the strategic goal of sustainable development, the resolution of environmental pollution and energy shortages through photocatalytic technology has become a topic of significant interest for scientists [1,2]. Against this backdrop, studies on various photocatalysts are underway, including those on ZnO-based and TiO2-based photocatalysts [3,4]. However, g-C3N4 has emerged as a promising photocatalyst, attracting significant attention from researchers due to its favorable cost-to-performance ratio, suitable light-driven band gap (2.70 eV) [5] and superior visible light response characteristics in recent years [6,7]. Nevertheless, as a single-component semiconductor photocatalyst, g-C3N4 is subject to the same inherent limitations as other semiconductor photocatalysts, including a small specific surface area, a high photo-generated carrier recombination rate, and low carrier mobility, which collectively restrict its photocatalytic activity [8,9]. Consequently, there is a pressing need to effectively modify g-C3N4 to overcome its shortcomings and enhance its photocatalytic performance [10,11].

Given the pivotal role that specific surface area plays in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts, researchers have devoted considerable attention to investigating the morphological modifications of g-C3N4 [12–14]. Among the methods of morphological modification of g-C3N4, the supramolecular precursor method has been identified as the most effective. The self-assembly of raw materials enables the fabrication of an array of supramolecular precursor structures with a substantial specific surface area. It is conceivable that the final g-C3N4 products, following calcination, may exhibit structural characteristics inherited from these precursor structures [15], including nanorods [16], nanospheres [17], nanoflowers [18], and nanotubes [19]. However, it is important to note that the initial morphology of the supramolecular precursor undergoing calcination to obtain g-C3N4 inevitably collapses to some extent at high calcination temperatures. This collapse prevents the complete inheritance of the initial morphology by the g-C3N4 product. Furthermore, an increase in specific surface area resulted in g-C3N4 nanostructures aggregating during photocatalytic reactions, thereby reducing light absorption and limiting the practical application of g-C3N4 in photocatalysis [20]. Fortunately, this issue could be addressed by enhancing the hydrogen bonding interactions between supermolecules in the precursor, thereby facilitating the formation of hydroxyl groups at the g-C3N4 molecular terminals subsequent to calcination [21]. The surface hydroxyl content was found to be closely related to the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 [22]. The presence of surface hydroxyl groups enabled the reaction with photo-generated holes, resulting in the production of hydroxyl radicals (
OH). Additionally, these groups demonstrated a positive impact on the chemisorption capacity of O2 molecules, facilitating the generation of anion radicals (
O
2
-
) on the surface of the g-C3N4 catalyst [23]. Therefore, the incorporation of hydroxyl groups into g-C3N4 molecules not only facilitates the inheritance of precursor morphology but also enhances the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess distinctive optical and electrical properties, including efficient light absorption, up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL), and rapid photo-generated electron transfer, which are derived from the conjugated π structures of CQDs [24]. Consequently, CQDs have been the subject of extensive investigation in composite photocatalysts in recent years. The formation of a composite photocatalyst comprising CQDs and g-C3N4 nanostructures enables the construction of heterojunctions between the two components through π-π interactions, which results in the effective photoelectric conversion characteristics and effective degradation of pollutants [25]. Furthermore, a considerable number of functional groups, including –OH and –COOH groups, are present on the surface of CQDs, which also enhances their photocatalytic activity [26–28]. The incorporation of OH-CQDs into composite photocatalysts results in the formation of molecular bonds between the surface hydroxyl functional groups of OH-CQDs and other nanostructured components. This results in an enhanced carrier transfer rate and a positive impact on the photocatalytic performance of the composite photocatalysts [29]. Furthermore, the incorporation of OH-CQDs into composite photocatalysts has been observed to augment the visible light absorption spectrum and mitigate the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the composite photocatalysts [30,31].

In view of the preceding summary, an investigation was initiated to examine the modification of g-C3N4 based composite photocatalysts. Hollow microspheres comprising g-C3N4 nanosheets decorated with OH-CQDs were prepared and their structural, optical, and photocatalytic properties were characterized. It was anticipated that the preparation of g-C3N4 with a specific morphology by the supramolecular precursor method would result in an enhancement of its specific surface area. Moreover, the incorporation of OH-CQDs was anticipated to facilitate more robust hydrogen bonding interactions between CQDs and g-C3N4 nanostructures, thereby enhancing the visible light absorption of g-C3N4. It was therefore anticipated that the modified g-C3N4 would exhibit enhanced photocatalytic performance. This study offers a novel perspective on the practical deployment of g-C3N4 in photocatalysis.

The synthesis of OH-CQDs was conducted via a hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.250 g of glucose and 12.50 mL of ethylene glycol were dissolved in 12.50 mL of deionized (DI) water. Following a 30 min of ultrasonic dispersion, the mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was stored in the dark at 4 °C for subsequent use.

Cyanuric acid and melamine solutions were prepared by separately dissolving 1.290 g of cyanuric acid and 1.260 g of melamine in 40 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Subsequently, a specific quantity of OH-CQDs was dispersed in the cyanuric acid solution. Following an ultrasonic dispersion period of 10 min, the aforementioned suspension was added to the melamine solution and stirred for 1 h, until white precipitate was formed in the suspension. Subsequently, the resulting precipitate was collected and washed with ethanol on several occasions, after which it was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h, thus obtaining a white powder. Subsequently, the white powder was heated in a muffle furnace in air at a rate of 2 °C/min, reaching a temperature of 550 °C and maintaining for period of 4 h. The final product was obtained, and the resulting sample was designated as “CCN-x” (where “x” represents the amount of OH-CQDs, which was 0, 0.50 mL, 1.00 mL, or 1.50 mL). For comparison, a bulk g-C3N4 sample was obtained by direct calcining melamine under the same temperature conditions and was designated as “CN”.

Melamine (C3H6N6, AR, 99.9 %) and cyanic acid (C3H3N3O3, AR, 99.9 %) were purchased from Tianjin Macklin Chemical Reagent Factory. Glucose (C6H12O6·H2O, AR, 99.5 %) was purchased from Tianjin Comio Chemical Reagent Factory. Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, AR, 99.9 %) and ethanol (C2H5OH, AR, 99.7 %) were purchased from Tianjin Kaixin Chemical Reagent Factory. Triethanolamine (TEOA, AR, 85 %) was purchased from Shanghai Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Rhodamine B (RhB, AR, 99.9 %) were purchased from China Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Isopropanol (IPA, AR, 99.7 %) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd. And the water used in the experiments was homemade distilled (DI) water. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification.
A comprehensive account of the testing process pertaining to the OH-CQD/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst can be accessed in the supporting information.

Fig. 1 depicts the field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) images of the samples. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a), the CN sample exhibited a bulk morphology comprising stacked lamellar structures. Fig. 1 (b-e) depicts the FESEM images of the CCN-0, CCN-0.5, CCN-1 and CCN-1.5 samples at a magnification of 10,000 times. It can be observed that the series samples exhibited a hollow flower-like morphology with a diameter of approximately 2.50 μm, which was formed by the spontaneous accumulation of g-C3N4 nanosheets. Furthermore, as the quantity of OH-CQDs incorporated into the samples increased, the flower-like morphology of the samples initially became more stereoscopic. Subsequently, the nanosheets on the flower-like structures gradually dispersed and formed flocculent structures. Ultimately, the flower-like structures were completely obliterated. It can thus be concluded that the control of the amount of OH-CQDs played a pivotal role in the formation of the g-C3N4 flower-like morphology observed in the composite samples. Fig. 1 (f, g) illustrates the images of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples magnified 20,000 times, demonstrating that the optimal decoration amount of OH-CQDs in the CCN-0.5 sample facilitated the formation of a more stereoscopic and complete flower-like morphology.

Fig. 2 illustrates the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements of the CCN-x series samples. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms depicted in Fig. 2 (a) indicate that all the samples exhibited type-IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, thereby implying the presence of a slit structure in the samples. These results are consistent with the aforementioned FESEM measurements of the samples. Fig. 2 (b) illustrates the pore size distribution curves of the samples. The pore size distributions of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples were found to be similar, with the majority of pores falling within the range of 10–60 nm. However, the CCN-0.5 sample exhibited the highest pore volume. Furthermore, the pore size distributions of the CCN-1 and CCN-1.5 samples were also similar, although their morphology exhibited notable differences, as illustrated in Fig. 1. It is evident that the CN sample exhibited the lowest pore volume. The specific surface area and average pore size values of the samples are presented in Table 1 . The specific surface area of the CCN-0.5 sample, which exhibited the smallest average pore size of 3.07 nm, was 11.30 times that of the CN sample.

Fig. 3 illustrates the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CCN-x series samples. All samples exhibit diffraction peaks at 13.3° and 27.3°, which correspond to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of g-C3N4, respectively [32]. In comparison to the CCN-0 sample, no peak-shift is observed in the other samples, indicating that decorating OH-CQDs on g-C3N4 nanostructures did not result in any disruption to the original crystal structure. Moreover, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) change of the (002) peak in the samples is presented in Table 2 . It demonstrates that the FWHM values of the samples first decreases and then increases with the increase of the decoration amount of OH-CQDs. The minimum value is observed in the CCN-0.5 sample, indicating that the appropriate amount of OH-CQDs decoration can improve the crystallinity of the final g-C3N4 product. In conjunction with the FESEM images presented in Fig. 1, it can be surmised that with the incremental increase in the quantity of OH-CQDs, the microsphere structures observed in the CCN-1 and CCN-1.5 samples were to some extent compromised, which in turn enhances the intensity of their (002) peaks. In light of these observations, it can be concluded that the OH-CQDs entering the g-C3N4 molecules have resulted in a smoother layered structure of g-C3N4, without altering the layer spacing. This is evidenced by the unchanged (002) peak intensity and the absence of peak-shift in the samples.

Fig. 4 illustrates the distribution of elements in the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples. Fig. 4 (a, b) depicts the local scanning areas of the samples employed for their element mapping. Fig. 4 (c-e) illustrates the distribution of C, N and O elements in the CCN-0 sample, while Fig. 4 (f-h) depicts the distribution of these elements in the CCN-0.5 sample. The O element in both the samples was residual from the cyanuric acid component in the precursor. The element mapping results demonstrate that the C, N and O elements were distributed uniformly throughout the samples, which lends considerable credibility to the subsequent X-rayphotoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements.

Fig. 5 depicts the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the samples. Fig. 5 (a) illustrates that the pure OH-CQDs sample, with an average size of approximately 2 nm, was distributed in a uniform manner. The red box-marked area demonstrates the lattice spacing of carbon quantum dots, which is observed as 0.230 nm in the upper right enlarged inset. This corresponds to the graphitic phase (100) crystal planes of OH-CQDs [33]. The top left inset illustrates the diffraction pattern of OH-CQDs. Thus, the successful preparation of OH-CQDs is confirmed. Fig. 5 (b) depicts the image of OH-CQDs decorated g-C3N4 at high magnification. The yellow circles indicate OH-CQDs, while the red arrows point to g-C3N4. It can be observed that OH-CQDs were uniformly distributed on the surface of the bulk phase g-C3N4, increasing the specific surface area of the composite and providing more reaction sites for the photocatalytic reaction [34]. Furthermore, the thickness of the g-C3N4 nanosheets is determined to be approximately 10 nm. Fig. 5 (c, d) illustrates the microscopic contour images of the nanostructures in the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples. In comparison to the CCN-0 sample, the CCN-0.5 sample exhibited a hollow microsphere structure comprising g-C3N4 nanosheets, which facilitates electron transfer [35].

To gain further insight into the variation in element binding states in the samples, XPS measurements were conducted on the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples, as presented in Fig. 6 and in Tables 3-5
. Fig. 6 (a) depicts the survey XPS spectra, while Fig. 6 (b-d) illustrate the elemental binding energy states of C1s, N1s and O1s in the samples, respectively. Some necessary data are presented in Table 3, which indicates that both CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples contained C, N, and O elements, which is consistent with the EDS results. The O and C contents in the CCN-0.5 sample were markedly elevated in comparison to those observed in the CCN-0 sample, thereby suggesting that OH-CQDs were effectively incorporated into the g-C3N4 molecules. Moreover, the C/N atomic ratio in the g-C3N4 molecules exhibited an increase from 1.05 in the CCN-0 sample to 1.08 in the CCN-0.5 sample, suggesting that the incorporation of OH-CQDs resulted in a reduction in the N content of the CCN-0.5 samples. It can thus be concluded that the N atoms in the terminal amino groups of g-C3N4 molecules were initially substituted when OH-CQDs were integrated into the g-C3N4 molecules. The C1s spectra of the samples illustrated in Fig. 6 (b) can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks, corresponding to the C–C/C=C and C–OH bonds in g-C3N4 molecules, as well as the N–C=N bonds in pollutant molecules [36]. These peaks are observed at 284.80 eV, 285.91 eV, and 288.29 eV in the CCN-0 sample, while in the CCN-0.5 sample, they appear at 284.80 eV, 285.91 eV, and 288.25 eV, respectively. Notably, the binding energy peak associated with the N–C=N bonds in the CCN-0.5 sample shifts towards a lower binding energy compared to that in the CCN-0 sample, indicating that the incorporation of OH-CQDs in the CCN-0.5 sample has introduced additional electrons to the g-C3N4 molecules. In the CCN-0.5 sample, the increased peak area ratio of C–C/C=C groups to N–C=N groups ((C–C/C=C)/N–C=N), as detailed in Table 4, indicates that the increased C content in the sample originated from OH-CQDs. Additionally, the enlarged peak area associated with the C-OH bonds in the CCN-0.5 sample provides a more direct validation of this finding. Fig. 6 (c) depicts the N1s binding states in the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples. In the CCN-0 sample, the observed peaks at 398.81 eV, 400.17 eV, 401.31 eV, and 404.13 eV correspond to the N1s binding energies of N-C=N groups, N-(C)3 groups, tri-s-triazine rings, and the positive charge effect, respectively. In contrast, the CCN-0.5 sample shows corresponding peaks at 398.77 eV, 400.13 eV, 401.27 eV, and 403.99 eV. The negative deviation of 0.1–0.2 eV is attributed to the influence of OH-CQDs on the chemical environment and surface electronic state of the g-C3N4 molecules [37]. The data related to the binding states of the N1s in the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples are shown in Table 5. It can be noted that the peak area ratio of N-(C)3 groups to N-C=N groups (N-(C)3/N-C=N) increased in the CCN-0.5 sample, confirming the successful incorporation of OH-CQDs into the CCN-0.5 sample. Fig. 6 (d) illustrates the O1s binding energy states in the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples, which can be deconvoluted into two distinct peaks, corresponding to C-O and C=O bonds. In the CCN-0 sample, these peaks are located at 533.32 eV and 533.60 eV, respectively, whereas in the CCN-0.5 sample, they appear at 532.23 eV and 533.58 eV. The data suggests that a small quantity of O atoms may have been retained from the supramolecular precursor in both samples. Additionally, it is evident that the O content in the CCN-0.5 sample is higher than in the CCN-0 sample, attributed to the incorporation of OH-CQDs. In the CCN-0.5 sample, the binding energy peaks for O1s and N1s shift towards a lower energy compared to the CCN-0 sample. This suggests that the Fermi level of OH-CQDs is higher than that of g-C3N4 in the samples that were prepared in this study. This finding supports the conclusions that were presented in the aforementioned paper [38]. Consequently, the introduction of OH-CQDs improved electron concentration of g-C3N4 crystals in the CCN-0.5 sample.

To ascertain the band structures of the CCN-x series samples and further elucidate their photocatalytic mechanism, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of the samples were characterized, as depicted in Fig. 7 (a). The absorption edge detected around 450 nm in the CCN-0 sample indicates the intrinsic absorption of g-C3N4, associated with electron transitions from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB). With the increasing incorporation of OH-CQDs in the CCN-x series samples, the overall absorption is intensified and experiences a red shift. This is because OH-CQDs serve as molecular centers for visible light absorption, thereby boosting the overall absorbance and enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency of the sample [24]. The optical band gaps of the samples, illustrated in Fig. 7 (b), are calculated by fitting with the Kubelka-Munk equation [39], which can be regarded as their band gaps. The usual formation of N vacancy defects generally results in a notable reduction of the band gap of g-C3N4 [40]. Nevertheless, the UV–vis DRS data provided here demonstrates that the band gap of the CCN-0.5 sample remained mostly consistent with that of the CCN-0 sample. This observation implies that the integration of OH-CQDs did not result in the generation of extra N vacancy defects within the g-C3N4 crystals. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (c, d), linear fits are applied to the Mott-Schottky plots to ascertain the flat band potentials of the samples through their intersection with the X-axis. The carrier density (Nd) in a semiconductor is generally inversely proportional to the slope of the fitted line on its Mott-Schottky plot. Therefore, by comparing the Nd values for CCN-0 and CCN-0.5, it becomes apparent that the carrier concentration in the CCN-0.5 sample is lower than that in the CCN-0 sample. In accordance with the XPS measurements illustrated in Fig. 1, the morphology of the CCN-0.5 sample exhibited enhancement relative to the CCN-0 sample. This improvement is attributed to the incorporation of OH-CQDs, which effectively diminished the terminal defect density in the CCN-0.5 sample. In addition, according to the previously mentioned data, the valence band maxima (VBM) for the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples are identified as 2.33 V and 2.35 V, respectively. The band structures of the CCN-0 and CCN-0.5 samples (relative to NHE, pH = 7) are illustrated in Fig. 7 (e), revealing that there is no obvious difference between them. It can thus be inferred that the decoration of an appropriate amount of OH-CQDs appears to have a negligible impact on the band structure of g-C3N4.

Fig. 8 (a) presents the photoluminescence (PL) spectra for the CCN-x series samples. The intrinsic emission peak of g-C3N4 is around 465 nm. With an increase in OH-CQDs content, the emission intensity of the CCN-x series samples progressively decreases, with all values being lower than that of the CCN-0 sample. This indicates that the photogenerated electrons within the g-C3N4 components were separated and transferred to the OH-CQDs components within the composite samples, thereby reducing their emission intensity. This separation behavior is beneficial for improving their photocatalytic performance. Fig. 8 (b) illustrates the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data in the high-frequency region for the CCN-x series specimens. Notably, the CCN-0.5 sample demonstrates the smallest semicircle radius, signifying the lowest impedance among the samples. Considering that the carrier concentration is reduced in the CCN-0.5 specimen relative to the CCN-0 specimen, it can be deduced that this sample possesses enhanced carrier mobility, thereby facilitating the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. In contrast, the CCN-1.5 sample demonstrates the most extensive semicircle radius, signifying the greatest impedance among the samples. Given that g-C3N4 molecules received electrons from OH-CQDs, the notable increase in impedance can only be ascribed to the decline in carrier mobility, which stemmed from the heightened concentration of electron-withdrawing terminal amino groups. As depicted in the FESEM images in Fig. 1, the hollow nanosphere structure of g-C3N4 was entirely compromised in the CCN-1.5 sample, resulting in numerous terminal defects within the g-C3N4 molecules. The photocurrent response of the CCN-x series samples is depicted in Fig. 8 (c), with the CCN-0.5 sample exhibiting the most pronounced response. This suggests that it exhibits the highest efficiency in separating photogenerated carriers compared to the other samples.

The photocatalytic characteristics of the samples were evaluated through the photocatalytic degradation of RhB solution using these samples under simulated visible light irradiation, with the findings presented in Fig. 9 . Fig. 9 (a) illustrates that all samples displayed varying degrees of photocatalytic activity, highlighting that g-C3N4 functions as an effective photocatalyst material responsive to visible light. Despite both the CN and CCN-0 samples being solely comprised of g-C3N4, the CCN-0 sample demonstrated superior photocatalytic performance compared to the bulk CN sample. This indicates that the CCN-0 sample possesses a greater density of active reaction sites. Furthermore, compared with the CCN-0 sample, the photocatalytic performance of the samples containing OH-CQDs were all improved, with the CCN-0.5 sample having the highest photocatalytic degradation capacity, which improved the efficiency of degrading RhB solution by 35.2 % over the bulk CN sample within 30 min. This suggesting that the modification of g-C3N4 with OH-CQDs results in a significant enhancement of its photocatalytic performance. Nevertheless, it was observed that the samples decorated with higher concentrations of OH-CQDs exhibited a reduction in their photocatalytic performance. This decline was attributed to a reduction in carrier mobility, which was caused by the disruption of the g-C3N4 morphology, as illustrated in the FESEM images in Fig. 1 (e). As a result, there was a reduction in carrier mobility, which contributed to the decline in photocatalytic performance of the samples that was observed. Fig. 9 (b) illustrates the time-dependent concentration of the RhB solution under the photocatalytic action of the CCN-0.5 sample over a 30 min period, thereby demonstrating its exceptional photocatalytic efficacy. Fig. 9 (c) illustrates the kinetic curves of the degradation of the RhB solution under the photocatalysis of the CCN-x series samples, derived from Fig. 9 (a), and the specific rate constant values are presented in Table 6 . The CCN-0.5 sample exhibited the highest rate constant. Moreover, the active groups involved in the photocatalytic process have been subjected to investigation. Triethanolamine (TEOA), isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ) and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were used as the scavengers of the active groups of vacancies (h+), hydroxyl radicals (
OH), superoxide radicals (
O
2
-
) and electrons (e−), respectively, and the contribution of the main reactive groups in the photocatalytic reaction of CCN-0.5 samples was then tested. Consequently, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (d), the order of contribution of the reactive groups in the degradation process was determined to be: h+ >
O
2
-
> e− >
OH. The specific reactions have been previously elucidated in our preceding research work [41]. The stability of the cyclic photocatalytic performance of the CCN-0.5 sample was further examined, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (e), which confirms that the CCN-0.5 sample exhibited high degradation performance after 6 cycles of photocatalytic degradation tests. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (f), the crystal structure of g-C3N4 in the CCN-0.5 sample was also examined through XRD measurements prior to and following the cyclic photocatalytic process. No notable changes were identified, indicating that the CCN-0.5 sample exhibits excellent stability and reusability in photocatalysis.

The generation of active groups during the photocatalytic degradation process was identified through the utilization of room temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy for the CCN-0.5 sample, and the response intensities of the four possible reactive groups, namely h+, e−,
OH and
O
2
-
, are presented in Fig. 10 . Fig. 10 (a, b) illustrates the generation of h+ and e− reactive groups following 30 min of simulated sunlight irradiation. Furthermore, Fig. 10 (c, d) demonstrates that the concentration of
OH and
O
2
-
, radicals was undetectable in the absence of light, but exhibited a significant increase following 30 min of sunlight irradiation. The EPR measurements are in accordance with the aforementioned results of the active group scavenger test, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (d).

Fig. 11 illustrates the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the CCN-0.5 sample. The flower-like morphology, which was formed by the stacking of g-C3N4 nanosheets, resulted in the repeated reflection of incident sunlight and improved light absorption. This contributed to the enhancement of the photocatalytic performance of the sample. The incident light excited the electrons from the VB to the CB of g-C3N4, which facilitated the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes in g-C3N4 by enabling the transfer of electrons to OH-CQDs through the interaction between hydrogen bonds, g-C3N4 crystals and CQDs. Moreover, the presence of OH-CQDs has been demonstrated to result in a shift of the VB of g-C3N4 to a more positive direction, albeit to a certain degree. thereby enhancing the reducing capacity and facilitating the photocatalytic degradation of contaminants [42].

A hollow flower-like g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst decorated with OH-CQDs was successfully prepared by a comprehensive approach involving the hydrothermal method, the supramolecular precursor method and the calcination method. The g-C3N4 samples prepared by the supramolecular precursor method exhibited a well-preserved hollow microsphere morphology, comprising nanosheets of a minimal thickness. The impact of OH-CQDs decoration on the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 was examined. The findings revealed that the incorporation of an optimal quantity of OH-CQDs into the precursors can enhance the microsphere structure of the final g-C3N4 product, elevate its crystalline quality, and improve the mobility of carriers. However, excessive introduction of OH-CQDs can disrupt the hollow microsphere structure of g-C3N4, thereby reducing its mobility. It was thus demonstrated that the appropriate amount of OH-CQD decoration optimized the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 by improving the quality of its crystals and facilitating the separation of photogenerated carriers. In the photocatalytic degradation of the RhB solution, the h+ groups were identified as the most active among the h+, e−,
OH, and
O
2
-
active groups. The study demonstrates that the introduction of OH-CQDs can effectively modify the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 based photocatalysts by facilitating the separation of photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, the identification of h+, e−,
OH, and
O
2
-
active groups during the degradation process indicates that the prepared composite photocatalyst possesses a wide application potential in the degradation of more organic pollutants in environmental water by means of the redox ability of these active groups.

Xin Tian: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianru Chen: Supervision, Data curation. Ruobing Jiang: Supervision. Haiyang Chen: Software. Jielin Zhang: Data curation. Jinlong Ren: Resources. Xihao Yu: Validation. Lina Liu: Investigation. Chenchen Feng: Formal analysis. Cuixia Li: Validation, Funding acquisition. Fucheng Yu: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Kechao Hu: Funding acquisition. Xiaogang Hou: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52362011and 22362020), Tamarisk Outstanding Young Talents Program of Lanzhou University of Technology in 2022 (No. 062211), Gansu Provincial Science Technology Plan (22JR5RA281), 2023 Project of the 14th Five-Year Plan for Education and Science of Gansu Province (GS [2023] GHB1370), Intellectual Property Plan Projects in Gansu Province (22ZSCQ052), Science and Technology Program of Gansu Province (24CXGA058), Gansu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (25JRRA067), and CAS “Light of West China” Program (2024).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2025.118289.
